ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic croissant entry (FOODON_03315091). No specific salted-egg-yolk-filled croissant entry exists; closest_parent match used and documented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers croissants generically. No filled-croissant or salted-egg-yolk-croissant sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy; closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for salted egg yolk croissant or filled croissant pastry as of 2026.

preparation_method: filled with salted egg yolk custard

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard salted egg yolk croissant weighs approximately 80 g, which is a typical single-serve portion sold at Singaporean bakeries and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Yolk Croissant — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per-100g nutrient fields. Values are accepted as-is. Saturated fat is estimated at ~16.5 g/100g based on the known butter-laminated croissant composition and salted egg yolk sauce; this sub-field is not separately reported in the HPB record and is derived from USDA FoodData Central croissant reference (FDC ID 174987) as a supporting estimate. Trans fat is estimated at ~0.2 g/100g consistent with commercial croissant production norms per USDA FoodData Central data.

gi_source.title: Croissant, plain — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg yolk croissant. GI of 67 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain croissant (GI ~67, medium category). The salted egg yolk filling adds fat and protein which may modestly lower the effective GI; however, the plain croissant GI is used as a conservative approved-source anchor. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (67) × available carbohydrate per serving (34.4 g/100g × 80 g serving / 100) / 100 = 67 × 27.52 / 100 ≈ 18.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 323 mg/100g combined with high saturated fat (~16.5 g/100g) and high sugar (11.0 g/100g with only 0.5 g/100g fibre) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit salted egg yolk croissant due to its sodium load (323 mg/100g), high saturated fat content, and high sugar content that promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; if consumed, restrict to a very small occasional portion and avoid pairing with other high-sodium or high-sugar foods on the same day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (34.4 g/100g) and sugar (11.0 g/100g) with minimal fibre (0.5 g/100g) driving rapid postprandial glucose excursions; high saturated fat (~16.5 g/100g) worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit salted egg yolk croissant because its high refined carbohydrate and sugar content with negligible fibre produces rapid postprandial glucose spikes (GI ~67, GL ~18 per 80 g serving), and its high saturated fat content further impairs insulin sensitivity; if consumed on rare occasions, pair with a high-fibre food and lean protein to partially attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~16.5 g/100g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100g from butter-laminated dough and salted egg yolk sauce, compounded by sodium 323 mg/100g and high sugar 11.0 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid salted egg yolk croissant due to its very high saturated fat content (~16.5 g/100g), the presence of trans fat (~0.2 g/100g from commercial croissant production), elevated sodium (323 mg/100g), and high sugar (11.0 g/100g), all of which collectively raise LDL cholesterol, promote arterial inflammation, and increase cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~16.5 g/100g exceeding the 5 g threshold and estimated trans fat ~0.2 g/100g acting as a hard pro-inflammatory disqualifier
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid salted egg yolk croissant entirely, especially during flares, as its very high saturated fat and trans fat content are strongly pro-inflammatory and no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrient is present to offset this risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted egg yolk croissant is a contemporary Singaporean fusion bakery item consisting of a buttery, flaky, laminated croissant filled with a rich salted duck egg yolk custard sauce. The croissant shell is made from butter-laminated wheat dough that is baked to produce a golden, crisp exterior with a soft, layered interior. The filling is a smooth, intensely savoury-sweet custard made from cured salted duck egg yolks blended with butter, sugar, and milk, producing a characteristic sandy, creamy texture and a bold umami-rich flavour profile. This item is popular across Singapore's artisan bakery scene and is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or indulgent snack. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 445 kcal per 100 g, with high total fat (30.34 g/100g, of which approximately 16.5 g is saturated fat from butter and egg yolk), moderate protein (8.5 g/100g), and significant refined carbohydrate (34.4 g/100g) with high sugar (11.0 g/100g) and very low dietary fibre (0.5 g/100g). Sodium is elevated at 323 mg/100g from the salted egg yolk curing process. The glycaemic index is approximately 67 (medium category, analogous to plain croissant), with a glycaemic load of approximately 18 per 80 g serving. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, refined carbohydrate, sugar, and sodium. It should be treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary item.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all reported fields. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central croissant reference data as a supporting secondary source, clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain croissant as the approved anchor.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific salted egg yolk croissant product are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item, and no matching entry exists in USDA FoodData Central for this exact product as of 2026-04-16; these fields are therefore marked as data not available per the approved sentinel value convention. Saturated fat (~16.5 g/100g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174987 (croissant, butter) as the closest approved-source analogue; these are estimates and should be replaced with direct measurement data when available. Halal status is marked false as salted duck egg yolk croissants from commercial Singapore bakeries may not carry halal certification; this should be verified per specific vendor. The dietary_flags.is_vegetarian field is set to true as the product contains no meat or seafood, though it contains egg and dairy.
